However need to avoid interfering with other system files. But we can run sed to delete lines containing HostbasedAuthentication from any .conf file. That matches both comments.

But review suggested we should use regex not matching comments.

The sed regex: 's/^[^#]*HostbasedAuthentication[[:space:]].*$/# HostbasedAuthentication \0/'? But we just need to remove lines. Use 'sed -i '/^[^#]*HostbasedAuthentication[[:space:]]/d' file'. That only deletes lines that start with non-# characters.

- Also for /etc/ssh/sshd_config, run sed.

Finally, create new file with 'HostbasedAuthentication no\n'. Use write_file (explicit newline). Set mode 0600, ownership root:root.

Also after modification, reload sshd.

But that uses '>>', which is allowed (no chaining). Provided we don't chain semicolons.

But ensure allow.

Better: run_cmd with 'printf "\nInclude /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf\n" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config'.

Ok.

We could use run_cmd 'sed -i ... /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf' but that uses glob; the shell will expand. We can run single command: sed -i '/^[^#]*HostbasedAuthentication[[:space:]]/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf; that uses the shell glob expansion. That's allowed since it's a single command. But the problem: if no files present, glob will pass literal pattern or error? In most shells, if no match, the pattern remains unchanged. Use -E? We'll assume there are files.

Since we might want to avoid messing with other conf files, but that's fine.

We still need to use sed to remove lines from sshd_config. So commands:

run_cmd "sed -i '/^[^#]*HostbasedAuthentication[[:space:]]/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config"

run_cmd "sed -i '/^[^#]*HostbasedAuthentication[[:space:]]/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf"

Then reload sshd: run_cmd 'systemctl reload sshd'.
